data.title: Australian Mining Delegation Opens the Market
data.description:
Toronto, Ontario--(Newsfile Corp. - March 4, 2026) - Shelley Jackson, Consul General and Trade and Investment Commissioner, Australian Consulate General, along with Western Australian Minister for Mines and Petroleum; Finance; Electoral Affairs; Goldfields-Esperance Hon. David Michael, and representatives from Australian mining companies, government and stakeholders, joined Luke Allshorn, Head of Business Development Australia and SE Asia, Toronto Stock Exchange (TSX) to open the market in celebration of the Prospectors & Developers Association of Canada (PDAC) 2026 Convention.

Cannot view this video? Visit:

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=RnHNFQkazSo

TSX and TSX Venture Exchange (TSXV) are home to more mining companies than any other global market, boasting a diverse range of public mining companies across various commodities and stages of development.

The PDAC is the leading voice of the mineral exploration and development community, an industry that supports 664,000 people in direct and indirect employment, and contributes $132 billion to Canada's GDP every year. Representing over 7,000 members around the world, PDAC's work centers on supporting a competitive, responsible, and sustainable mineral sector. The PDAC is the leading voice of the mineral exploration and development community. The mission of PDAC is to promote a globally-responsible, vibrant and sustainable mineral sector that encourages leading practices in technical, operational, environmental, safety and social performance.

The convention brings together an ever-growing audience of more than 27,000 attendees from over 125 countries for its educational programming, networking events, business opportunities and fun.

MEDIA CONTACT:

Catherine Kee

Head of Media Relations, TMX Group

catherine.kee@tmx.com

data.title: US crude stocks and distillate inventories rise, gasoline inventories fall, EIA says
data.description:
By Liz Hampton

U.S. crude stocks and distillate inventories rose while gasoline inventories fell last week, the Energy Information Administration said on Wednesday.

Crude inventories rose by 3.5 million barrels to 439.3 million barrels in the week ended February 27, the EIA said, compared with analysts' expectations in a Reuters poll for a 2.3 million-barrel rise.

Crude stocks at the Cushing, Oklahoma, delivery hub (USOICC=ECI) rose by 1.6 million barrels in the week, the EIA said.

Oil futures remained in negative territory following the report. Global Brent crude futures were trading at $80.86 a barrel, down 54 cents, while U.S. West Texas Intermediate futures were off 36 cents to $74.20 a barrel.

Refinery crude runs (USOICR=ECI) rose by 180,000 barrels per day, the EIA said, while utilization rates (USOIRU=ECI) rose by 0.6 percentage points in the week to 89.2%.

U.S. gasoline stocks (USOILG=ECI) fell by 1.7 million barrels in the week to 253.1 million barrels, the EIA said, compared with analysts' expectations in a Reuters poll for a 784,000-barrel draw.​

Distillate stockpiles (USOILD=ECI), which include diesel and heating oil, rose by 429,000 barrels in the week to 120.8 million barrels, versus expectations for a 2.6 million-barrel drop, the EIA data showed.

Net U.S. crude imports (USOICI=ECI) fell last week by 19,000 barrels per day, EIA said.

data.title: Bond Investors Focus on Energy Inflation as Middle East Conflict Rages — Interview
data.description:
(15:34 GMT) *Inflation Risks Push Bond Yields Higher, Override Safe-Haven Appeal, BNY Says
(15:34 GMT) *Investors Focus on Likely Duration of Middle East Conflict, Severity of Disruption, BNY Says
(15:35 GMT) *Transmission Channel From Energy Prices to Inflation Is Direct and Well-Established, BNY Says
(15:35 GMT) Bond Investors Focus on Energy Inflation as Middle East Conflict Rages — Interview

By Emese Bartha

The prospect of the conflict in the Middle East pushing energy prices higher and fueling inflation is the primary concern for fixed-income investors, which is causing yields on sovereign bonds to rise, Jason Granet, chief investment officer of BNY, said.

Concerns about inflation, which could delay interest-rate cuts by the Federal Reserve, are overriding the appeal of sovereign bonds as safe havens. The key focus meanwhile is on how long the conflict will last and what its outcome will be.

The 10-year Treasury yield peaked at 4.117% on Tuesday, the highest level since Feb. 13, according to Tradeweb, before dropping back. It last traded at 4.084% on Wednesday, up 2.9 basis points on the day.

"The market response is currently being driven primarily by concerns about energy-related inflation rather than by traditional flight-to-quality behavior," he said.

"Even if the investor community hopes for a relatively swift resolution, expectations vary widely regarding both the duration and potential severity of the disruption."

The U.S. and Israel launched attacks on the Iranian regime on Saturday, killing several high-ranking Iranian leaders, including Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamanei.

Iran, in response, launched air attacks against Gulf Arabic countries and threatened the closure of the Strait of Hormuz, a key naval route for oil tankers.

U.S. President Trump said Tuesday the U.S. might escort tankers through the Strait of Hormuz to ensure the flow of oil.

"Markets are watching events around the Strait of Hormuz with significant focus, given its central role in global energy supply," Granet said.

That uncertainty feeds directly into risk premia on bonds, he said. The possibility of a more prolonged conflict that would impact energy supply "is reflected very quickly in inflation markets."

The transmission channel from energy prices to inflation fixings is well established, and the link is direct, he said.

The price of a barrel of Brent crude oil jumped as high as $85.12 on Tuesday, its highest since mid-2024, having closed at $72.48 on Friday, before the U.S. attack on Iran. Brent last traded at $81.09.

"Even the possibility of sustained pressure on commodity prices is enough to push inflation expectations higher, regardless of how the geopolitical situation ultimately evolves," Granet said.

Despite massive selloffs in U.S. Treasurys and other global bonds on Monday and Tuesday, government-bond yields remained well below levels seen in December and January, having fallen significantly last month. Yields rise when investors sell bonds.

"It is worth noting that interest-rate markets and most other fixed-income markets remain within the ranges observed in recent months," Granet said. "Outside commodity markets, we have not seen a break into a fundamentally new regime."

The key issue for portfolio managers is the length of the conflict as they assess whether recent moves are likely to be temporary or more enduring.

"That distinction is extremely challenging: the situation is evolving rapidly, the relevant data is noisy, and market narratives are shifting at speed," Granet said.

As of December 31, 2025, BNY oversaw $2.2 trillion in assets under management and $59 trillion in assets under custody and/or administration.

Write to Emese Bartha at emese.bartha@wsj.com

data.title: New Zealand deploys two defence planes to the Middle East in preparation for evacuations
data.description:
The New Zealand government said on Wednesday that it would send two Defence Force C-130J Hercules aircraft to the Middle East in case they were needed for evacuating New Zealanders from the region.

New Zealanders are among the tens of thousands of travellers across the Middle East, who have become stranded after escalating conflict between the U.S., Israel and Iran disrupted flights worldwide and forced the closure of major airports including Dubai, the world's ​busiest international hub.

The government said there were currently around 3000 New Zealanders registered as living in the Middle East.

“The events in the Middle East are fast-moving, dangerous and complex - and our primary focus is on assisting New Zealanders in the region to the extent possible in this very challenging security environment,” Foreign Minister Winston Peters said in a statement.

He added that sending two planes along with consular staff was so "they can be ready when conditions allow to assist with any civilian evacuation operations."

Peters added that with airspace closed and most commercial flights not operating, the government continues to recommend that New Zealanders shelter in place - or take any safe and practical opportunities that are available to leave the region.

The statement said that decisions on where the consular response team and the two Hercules would be deployed were still being made.

data.title: LME fines PAC Global Services Spain 250,000 pounds for warehouse violations
data.description:
The London Metal Exchange fined warehouse operator PAC Global Services Spain (PGS) 250,000 pounds ($334,175) in a disciplinary action due to breaches of its rules, the LME said on Wednesday.

The exchange, the world's oldest and largest market for industrial metals, listed eight violations of its warehouse agreement by PGS in a members' notice.

The most serious violation was found during an investigation of a PGS warehouse in Taiwan, where it found copper stored in an open yard outside the facility.

"The storage of metal on warrant outside of an LME approved shed is an egregious breach of the warehouse agreement ... and as such the financial penalty reflects this," the notice said.

PGS runs 39 LME-registered warehouses in Europe and Asia, according to the LME website.

The LME is owned by Hong Kong Exchanges and Clearing Ltd.

($1 = 0.7481 pounds)

data.title: Czech Defense Company CSG to Build Slovak Artillery Propellant Plant Under JV
data.description:
By Cristina Gallardo

Czech defense company CSG Group said it would build an artillery propellant plant in Slovakia after forming a joint venture with France's Eurenco.

CSG said Wednesday that ammunition company ZVS Holding, which it partly owns, will form a joint venture with Eurenco, a French manufacturer of explosives, propellant and military fuels.

The partnership aims to build an artillery propellant plant in the eastern Slovakian town of Strazske, with an investment of around 300 million euro ($348.4 million), the Amsterdam-listed company said. Construction of the plant should start in the near future, with production expected to kick off in 2028, CSG added.

The move comes as arms makers all over Europe take steps to increase production of ammunition amid greater governmental demand, as European countries seek to replenish their stocks.

However, European defense groups have been constrained by a shortage of propellant charge systems in their efforts to produce artillery ammunition.

Write to Cristina Gallardo at cristina.gallardo@wsj.com

data.title: Chamath Palihapitiya Warns There's A Structural Failing In BTC – ‘Bitcoin Fails On Those Two Dimensions’
data.description:
Key points:
  • He said Bitcoin has a "structural failing" because it can't serve as a reserve asset for central banks. 
  • The investor also noted that Bitcoin will likely remain an asset held mostly through ETFs and private portfolios rather than by government institutions because it runs on a public ledger that anyone can see.
  • Palihapitiya said that stablecoins are a "structural innovation" that makes payments easier and helps expand global transaction rails.

Chamath Palihapitiya, a billionaire investor and CEO of Social Capital, said that Bitcoin fails on two dimensions that could make it harder for central banks to use it in the long term. 

The investor told Zerodha co-founder Nikhil Kamath in an interview that for a crypto asset to be widely used at the national level, it needs features that allow central banks to hold it as part of their reserves. “There’s a structural failing in Bitcoin,” he said.

“One is fungibility, and two is privacy. And so Bitcoin fails on those two dimensions,” he said, arguing that the limitations could prevent the cryptocurrency from becoming “a structural holding of a central bank.”

Palihapitiya said that Bitcoin's public ledger is hard to keep private because it is viewable by everyone. Because of this, he said that Bitcoin will probably stay an asset that investors hold through ETFs and private portfolios, and will not be held as a reserve asset by central banks.

Bitcoin (BTC) was trading at $72,762 on Wednesday morning, up over 8% in the last 24 hours. On Stocktwits, retail sentiment around BTC improved from ‘bullish’ to ‘extremely bullish’ zone over the past day, as chatter levels remained at ‘low’ over the past day.

Stablecoins Reduce Payment Friction

The venture capitalist noted that other parts of the crypto ecosystem still look promising. He called stablecoins a "structural innovation" that makes payments easier and could help people do business around the world. He also said that stablecoins reduce “friction and allow transaction rails to proliferate,” making them useful for improving the efficiency of global payments.

Chamath Palihapitiya has been a well-known figure in the crypto world for years. He was one of the first people to buy Bitcoin, reportedly acquiring it when it was worth about $80. He has called Bitcoin "gold 2.0" over the years and said it could protect against inflation and unstable money.

During the boom in Special Purpose Acquisition Companies (SPACs) from 2020 to 2021, Palihapitiya became known as the "SPAC King." Through his company, Social Capital, he started and funded several high-profile SPAC deals that made companies like Virgin Galactic, Opendoor, Clover Health, and SoFi public.

data.title: TSX rises on tech, mining strength
data.description:
Key points:
  • TSX up 0.33%
  • Tech leads gains, Bitfarms rises 9%
  • Retailer Pet Valu falls 4.4%

By Utkarsh Hathi

Canada's main stock index edged higher on Wednesday, lifted by mining and tech shares, while investors weighed a report that Iranian operatives reached out to the U.S. to pursue talks to end the conflict.

The S&P/TSX composite index was up 0.33% at 33,896.21 points, as of 10:11 a.m. ET. The index nearly fell 4% in the previous session before recovering, as fears of inflation rippled across global markets.

"The market is expecting things to de-escalate. We've seen multiple global conflicts impacting the availability of oil over the last several years. Throughout all that, we've come through with a reasonably strong global economy, measured oil prices and good stock performance," said Josh Sheluk, portfolio manager at Verecan Capital Management.

Tech stocks led the sectoral gains with a 2.6% jump, as blockchain farm operator Bitfarms climbed 9% after bitcoin prices hit more than a three-week high.

The gold index (.SPTTGD) rose 0.5% as spot gold gained 1.4%. On the broader materials sector, base metal miners gained 0.6% as silver and copper (HGc1) prices also climbed.

The heavily weighted financial index (.SPTTFS) rose 0.5%.

Energy shares traded 0.6% lower as oil prices eased, retreating from gains made early in the day amid the Middle East conflict.

Iranian intelligence operatives indirectly contacted the CIA a day after the attacks, but U.S. officials remain skeptical that either the Trump administration or Iran is prepared for a near-term de-escalation, the New York Times reported.

President Donald Trump said on Tuesday the U.S. Navy could begin escorting tankers through the Strait of Hormuz if necessary.

Among individual movers, shares of pet-food retailer Pet Valu fell 4.4% after multiple brokerages cut its price target. The broader consumer discretionary subindex (.GSPTTCD) fell 0.2%.

Shares of George Weston fell 3.1% after the food and drug retailer missed fourth-quarter revenue estimates, sending the consumer staples sector (.GSPTTCS) down 0.7%.

  Imports (line 21) Stock Change (line 24) + Adjustment (line 25). 
9 See Table 2, footnote #3. 
10 Other Oil Product Supplied = Total Product Supplied (line 26) less the product supplied of Finished Motor Gasoline 
  (line 27), Kerosene-Type Jet Fuel (line 28), Distillate Fuel Oil (line 29), Residual Fuel Oil (line 30), and 
  Propane/Propylene (line 31).
